{{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:かとつけ}} [[Category:日本の年中行事]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:演芸]] [[Category:大道芸]] [[Category:日本の伝統芸能]] [[Category:門付|*]]

[[ファイル:Bunraku_kugutsu.png|thumb|280px|傀儡師（[[傀儡子]]）の人形回しのイメージ。]] '''門付'''（かどづけ）は、日本の[[大道芸]]の一種で、門口に立ち行い金品を受け取る形式の[[日本伝統芸能|芸能]]の総称であり、およびそれを行う者の総称である<ref name="hyakka_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[世界大百科事典|世界大百科事典 第2版]]、[[コトバンク]]、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="maipe_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[百科事典マイペディア]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="jisen_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[デジタル大辞泉]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="jirin_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[大辞林|大辞林 第三版]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>。表記は'''門付け'''（かどづけ）、読みは「かどつけ」とも<ref name="jisen_門付" />。'''門付する'''（かどづけする）という動詞としても使用し<ref name="jisen_門付" /><ref name="jirin_門付" />、芸については'''門付芸'''（かどづけげい）、行う者については'''門付芸人'''（かどづけげいにん）ともいう。多くは[[予祝芸能]]である。
== 歴史と概要 == 門付の発祥の根本には、季節に応じて神が祝福に訪れるという[[民間信仰]]があった<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" />。「祝言人」（ほかいびと）の芸能に由来するとも言われ、これは神を装い、民家の戸口等に立って祝福することば「祝い言」（ほかいごと）を発することで金銭を乞う者である<ref name="maipe_門付" />。[[平安時代]]、934年（[[承平 (日本)|承平]]4年）ころに成立したとされる『[[和名類聚抄]]』（934年ころ成立）には、「乞児」（ほかいびと）の文字で解説されており、[[乞食|物乞い]]であると10世紀の時点で定義されている<ref name="jisen_乞児">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>。
[[室町時代]]（[[14世紀]] - [[16世紀]]）には、寺に属しあるいは没落して民間に流れた職業芸人である[[声聞師]]（しょうもじ）が行った[[読経]]や[[曲舞]]等の芸能や<ref name="hyakka_声聞師">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、神社に隷属して雑役を行っていた下級の[[神人]]<ref name="hyakka_神人">{{Kotobank|神人|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>たちが、一定の季節に各戸を回って行っていた芸能である<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。これも次第に転落して、物乞いになっていった<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。この時代には、新春の予祝芸能である「[[千秋万歳]]」を声聞師らが行い<ref name="maipe_千秋万歳">{{Kotobank|千秋万歳|2=百科事典マイペディア}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく「[[松囃子]]」を声聞師のほか若党（[[武家奉公人]]）、[[町女房]]つまり一般町人の女性らが行い、平安時代、[[9世紀]]には存在したという漂流民「[[傀儡子]]」たちが、「[[人形回し]]」（操り人形による[[人形劇]]）等を行っていた<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。
[[ファイル:Manzai by unknown artist - wittig collection.jpg|thumb|200px|[[19世紀]]の「[[萬歳]]」のスタイル。]] [[江戸時代]]（[[17世紀]] - [[19世紀]]）には、正月に門口や座敷でその一家の予祝の祝言を謡う「[[萬歳]]」<ref name="hyakka_万歳">{{Kotobank|万歳|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく[[養蚕]]の予祝の祝言を謡う「[[春駒]]」<ref name="hyakka_春駒">{{Kotobank|春駒|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく[[農耕]]の予祝の祝言を謡う「鳥追」（[[鳥追い]]）<ref name="hyakka_春駒" /><ref name="jisen_鳥追い">{{Kotobank|鳥追い|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>等の門付歌の類が広まった<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" />。萬歳は、千秋万歳の流れを汲むものであり<ref name="hyakka_万歳" />、鳥追は、江戸初期（17世紀）に京都・[[悲田院]]の[[与次郎]]（[[非人|非人頭]]のこと）が始めたとされ、江戸中期以降には、[[女太夫]]（[[非人]]、女性芸人）たちが[[日和下駄]]に[[編み笠]]のスタイルで[[三味線]]を弾きながら「鳥追唄」を歌いながらの門付を行うようになった<ref name="jisen_鳥追い" />。
正月の時期の門付は豊富で、[[大黒天]]の面・頭巾をかぶり、[[お福]]や[[えびす]]を伴って現れる「[[大黒舞]]」<ref name="hyakka_大黒舞">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、『[[古事記]]』にも記述がある古い芸能である「[[獅子舞]]」<ref name="hyakka_獅子舞">{{Kotobank|獅子舞|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、京都では巨大な張子、江戸では[[福禄寿]]の扮装をして歌い祝詞をあげる[[ちょろけん]]<ref name="jisen_ちょろけん">{{Kotobank|ちょろけん|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>といった、神々の姿をかたどったいでたちによるものがあり、現代でも一部地方では、これらの芸能は継承されている。
正月以外の時期の門付としては、[[節分]]・[[大晦日]]に厄年の人の家の門口に立った門付芸としての「[[厄払い]]」<ref name="jisen_厄払い">{{Kotobank|厄払い|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、[[師走]]に現れる芸人集団による「[[節季候]]」<ref name="hyakka_節季候">{{Kotobank|節季候|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、季節を問わず事件を詠み込み三味線で歌った「[[歌祭文]]」<ref name="jirin_歌祭文">{{Kotobank|歌祭文|2=大辞林 第三版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、[[空也]]が始めたものを真似た「[[鉢叩]]」<ref name="hyakka_鉢叩">{{Kotobank|鉢叩|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>等が江戸時代を通じて広がった。町家のどこかで吉凶慶事があると集まるので、あまり来られて困る場合は、非人頭の善七または松右衛門の判が押してある「仕切り札」を門口に貼った<ref>[{{NDLDC|1042111/125}} 『江戸ばなし. 其2』][[三田村鳶魚]]、大東出版社、昭和18</ref>。門付は非人が行なっていたため、これが貼ってある場合は門付しないという仲間内の決まりになっていた。
[[明治|明治時代]]に新しく始まった門付に、「[[法界屋]]」が挙げられる<ref name="jisen_法界屋">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月9日閲覧。</ref>。明治中期（[[1890年代]]ころ）に編み笠をかぶった[[書生]]が[[月琴]]に合わせて『[[法界節]]』を街頭で歌う[[巷間芸能]]が始められ、明治末期（[[1900年代]]ころ）には、大阪を中心に、[[琴]]・三味線・[[胡弓]]・[[尺八]]・[[太鼓]]といった多数の奏者とともに歌う門付となり、流行した<ref name="jisen_法界屋" />。また、[[沖縄]]でも、「京太郎」（[[チョンダラー]]）という門付があり、「萬歳」を詠い、本土の「傀儡子」のように「仏」（フトウキ）と呼ばれる人形回しを行い、門付した<ref name="maipe_チョンダラー">{{Kotobank|チョンダラー|2=百科事典マイペディア}}、2012年8月9日閲覧。</ref>。
門付の文化は、民間信仰に端を発したその発祥の時代にあっては、下級の宗教者が携わったが、次第に宗教者の行なう儀式や芸能を模倣して、市民生活の外側に置かれた貧しい者が担うようになった<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。その中から、『[[四つ竹節]]』<ref>楽器としての四ツ竹は、寄席に出演する江戸売り声の宮田章司が使用している</ref>や『法界節』のような[[俗謡]]が生まれている。{{See|#俗謡}} 門付の演じた夷・大黒天・お福・ちょろけん等のキャラクターは、京都の[[伏見人形]]をはじめとする各地の[[土人形]]にそのモチーフを提供した。
== おもな一覧 == 総称を列挙し、地域独特の呼称・習慣等は省く<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" /><ref name="jisen_門付" /><ref name="jirin_門付" />。途絶えたものもあれば、これらの発展形が、門付ではない形で現在も各地民間に受け継がれているものもある。 * [[千秋万歳]] * [[松囃子]] * [[傀儡子]]（[[傀儡]]、[[人形回し]]、[[でこまわし]]） * [[萬歳]] - 門付歌 * [[春駒]] - 門付歌 * [[鳥追い|鳥追]] - 門付歌 * [[大黒舞]] * [[獅子舞]] * [[物吉]] * [[猿回し]] * [[ちょろけん]] * [[夷回し]]（[[夷舁き]]） * [[太々神楽]] * [[鹿島の事触れ]] * [[厄払い]] * [[夷舞]] * [[節季候]] * [[門浄瑠璃]] * [[門説経]] - 門付歌であり、[[説経節]]を門付芸として行うもの * [[歌祭文]] - 門付歌 * [[鉢叩]] - 門付歌 * [[なまいだ坊主]] * [[願人坊主]]（[[すたすた坊主]]） * [[芥太夫]] * [[簓摺り]] * [[法界屋]] * [[箱回し]] * [[化他]] * [[ちょぼくれ]] * [[胸叩き]] * [[チョンダラー]] - 沖縄の門付 * [[春田打ち]]
== 俗謡 == * [[四つ竹節]] * [[間の山節]] * [[博多節]] * [[串本節]] * [[新保広大寺]] * [[踊口説]] * [[法界節]] * [[新内節]]

== 外部リンク == * {{Kotobank|門付|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}} * {{Kotobank|門付|2=百科事典マイペディア}} * {{Kotobank|門付|2=デジタル大辞泉}} * {{Kotobank|門付|2=大辞林 第三版}}
{{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:かとつけ}} [[Category:日本の年中行事]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:演芸]] [[Category:大道芸]] [[Category:日本の伝統芸能]] [[Category:門付|*]]

[[ファイル:Bunraku_kugutsu.png|thumb|280px|傀儡師（[[傀儡子]]）の人形回しのイメージ。]] '''門付'''（かどづけ）は、日本の[[大道芸]]の一種で、門口に立ち行い金品を受け取る形式の[[日本伝統芸能|芸能]]の総称であり、およびそれを行う者の総称である<ref name="hyakka_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[世界大百科事典|世界大百科事典 第2版]]、[[コトバンク]]、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="maipe_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[百科事典マイペディア]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="jisen_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[デジタル大辞泉]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="jirin_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[大辞林|大辞林 第三版]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>。表記は'''門付け'''（かどづけ）、読みは「かどつけ」とも<ref name="jisen_門付" />。'''門付する'''（かどづけする）という動詞としても使用し<ref name="jisen_門付" /><ref name="jirin_門付" />、芸については'''門付芸'''（かどづけげい）、行う者については'''門付芸人'''（かどづけげいにん）ともいう。多くは[[予祝芸能]]である。
== 歴史と概要 == 門付の発祥の根本には、季節に応じて神が祝福に訪れるという[[民間信仰]]があった<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" />。「祝言人」（ほかいびと）の芸能に由来するとも言われ、これは神を装い、民家の戸口等に立って祝福することば「祝い言」（ほかいごと）を発することで金銭を乞う者である<ref name="maipe_門付" />。[[平安時代]]、934年（[[承平 (日本)|承平]]4年）ころに成立したとされる『[[和名類聚抄]]』（934年ころ成立）には、「乞児」（ほかいびと）の文字で解説されており、[[乞食|物乞い]]であると10世紀の時点で定義されている<ref name="jisen_乞児">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>。
[[室町時代]]（[[14世紀]] - [[16世紀]]）には、寺に属しあるいは没落して民間に流れた職業芸人である[[声聞師]]（しょうもじ）が行った[[読経]]や[[曲舞]]等の芸能や<ref name="hyakka_声聞師">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、神社に隷属して雑役を行っていた下級の[[神人]]<ref name="hyakka_神人">{{Kotobank|神人|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>たちが、一定の季節に各戸を回って行っていた芸能である<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。これも次第に転落して、物乞いになっていった<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。この時代には、新春の予祝芸能である「[[千秋万歳]]」を声聞師らが行い<ref name="maipe_千秋万歳">{{Kotobank|千秋万歳|2=百科事典マイペディア}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく「[[松囃子]]」を声聞師のほか若党（[[武家奉公人]]）、[[町女房]]つまり一般町人の女性らが行い、平安時代、[[9世紀]]には存在したという漂流民「[[傀儡子]]」たちが、「[[人形回し]]」（操り人形による[[人形劇]]）等を行っていた<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。
[[ファイル:Manzai by unknown artist - wittig collection.jpg|thumb|200px|[[19世紀]]の「[[萬歳]]」のスタイル。]] [[江戸時代]]（[[17世紀]] - [[19世紀]]）には、正月に門口や座敷でその一家の予祝の祝言を謡う「[[萬歳]]」<ref name="hyakka_万歳">{{Kotobank|万歳|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく[[養蚕]]の予祝の祝言を謡う「[[春駒]]」<ref name="hyakka_春駒">{{Kotobank|春駒|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく[[農耕]]の予祝の祝言を謡う「鳥追」（[[鳥追い]]）<ref name="hyakka_春駒" /><ref name="jisen_鳥追い">{{Kotobank|鳥追い|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>等の門付歌の類が広まった<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" />。萬歳は、千秋万歳の流れを汲むものであり<ref name="hyakka_万歳" />、鳥追は、江戸初期（17世紀）に京都・[[悲田院]]の[[与次郎]]（[[非人|非人頭]]のこと）が始めたとされ、江戸中期以降には、[[女太夫]]（[[非人]]、女性芸人）たちが[[日和下駄]]に[[編み笠]]のスタイルで[[三味線]]を弾きながら「鳥追唄」を歌いながらの門付を行うようになった<ref name="jisen_鳥追い" />。
正月の時期の門付は豊富で、[[大黒天]]の面・頭巾をかぶり、[[お福]]や[[えびす]]を伴って現れる「[[大黒舞]]」<ref name="hyakka_大黒舞">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、『[[古事記]]』にも記述がある古い芸能である「[[獅子舞]]」<ref name="hyakka_獅子舞">{{Kotobank|獅子舞|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、京都では巨大な張子、江戸では[[福禄寿]]の扮装をして歌い祝詞をあげる[[ちょろけん]]<ref name="jisen_ちょろけん">{{Kotobank|ちょろけん|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>といった、神々の姿をかたどったいでたちによるものがあり、現代でも一部地方では、これらの芸能は継承されている。
正月以外の時期の門付としては、[[節分]]・[[大晦日]]に厄年の人の家の門口に立った門付芸としての「[[厄払い]]」<ref name="jisen_厄払い">{{Kotobank|厄払い|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、[[師走]]に現れる芸人集団による「[[節季候]]」<ref name="hyakka_節季候">{{Kotobank|節季候|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、季節を問わず事件を詠み込み三味線で歌った「[[歌祭文]]」<ref name="jirin_歌祭文">{{Kotobank|歌祭文|2=大辞林 第三版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、[[空也]]が始めたものを真似た「[[鉢叩]]」<ref name="hyakka_鉢叩">{{Kotobank|鉢叩|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>等が江戸時代を通じて広がった。町家のどこかで吉凶慶事があると集まるので、あまり来られて困る場合は、非人頭の善七または松右衛門の判が押してある「仕切り札」を門口に貼った<ref>[{{NDLDC|1042111/125}} 『江戸ばなし. 其2』][[三田村鳶魚]]、大東出版社、昭和18</ref>。門付は非人が行なっていたため、これが貼ってある場合は門付しないという仲間内の決まりになっていた。
[[明治|明治時代]]に新しく始まった門付に、「[[法界屋]]」が挙げられる<ref name="jisen_法界屋">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月9日閲覧。</ref>。明治中期（[[1890年代]]ころ）に編み笠をかぶった[[書生]]が[[月琴]]に合わせて『[[法界節]]』を街頭で歌う[[巷間芸能]]が始められ、明治末期（[[1900年代]]ころ）には、大阪を中心に、[[琴]]・三味線・[[胡弓]]・[[尺八]]・[[太鼓]]といった多数の奏者とともに歌う門付となり、流行した<ref name="jisen_法界屋" />。また、[[沖縄]]でも、「京太郎」（[[チョンダラー]]）という門付があり、「萬歳」を詠い、本土の「傀儡子」のように「仏」（フトウキ）と呼ばれる人形回しを行い、門付した<ref name="maipe_チョンダラー">{{Kotobank|チョンダラー|2=百科事典マイペディア}}、2012年8月9日閲覧。</ref>。
門付の文化は、民間信仰に端を発したその発祥の時代にあっては、下級の宗教者が携わったが、次第に宗教者の行なう儀式や芸能を模倣して、市民生活の外側に置かれた貧しい者が担うようになった<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。その中から、『[[四つ竹節]]』<ref>楽器としての四ツ竹は、寄席に出演する江戸売り声の宮田章司が使用している</ref>や『法界節』のような[[俗謡]]が生まれている。{{See|#俗謡}} 門付の演じた夷・大黒天・お福・ちょろけん等のキャラクターは、京都の[[伏見人形]]をはじめとする各地の[[土人形]]にそのモチーフを提供した。
== おもな一覧 == 総称を列挙し、地域独特の呼称・習慣等は省く<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" /><ref name="jisen_門付" /><ref name="jirin_門付" />。途絶えたものもあれば、これらの発展形が、門付ではない形で現在も各地民間に受け継がれているものもある。 * [[千秋万歳]] * [[松囃子]] * [[傀儡子]]（[[傀儡]]、[[人形回し]]、[[でこまわし]]） * [[萬歳]] - 門付歌 * [[春駒]] - 門付歌 * [[鳥追い|鳥追]] - 門付歌 * [[大黒舞]] * [[獅子舞]] * [[物吉]] * [[猿回し]] * [[ちょろけん]] * [[夷回し]]（[[夷舁き]]） * [[太々神楽]] * [[鹿島の事触れ]] * [[厄払い]] * [[夷舞]] * [[節季候]] * [[門浄瑠璃]] * [[門説経]] - 門付歌であり、[[説経節]]を門付芸として行うもの * [[歌祭文]] - 門付歌 * [[鉢叩]] - 門付歌 * [[なまいだ坊主]] * [[願人坊主]]（[[すたすた坊主]]） * [[芥太夫]] * [[簓摺り]] * [[法界屋]] * [[箱回し]] * [[化他]] * [[ちょぼくれ]] * [[胸叩き]] * [[チョンダラー]] - 沖縄の門付 * [[春田打ち]]

{{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:かとつけ}} [[Category:門付|*]]

[[ファイル:Bunraku_kugutsu.png|thumb|280px|傀儡師（[[傀儡子]]）の人形回しのイメージ。]] '''門付'''（かどづけ）は、日本の[[大道芸]]の一種で、門口に立ち行い金品を受け取る形式の[[日本伝統芸能|芸能]]の総称であり、およびそれを行う者の総称である<ref name="hyakka_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[世界大百科事典|世界大百科事典 第2版]]、[[コトバンク]]、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="maipe_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[百科事典マイペディア]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="jisen_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[デジタル大辞泉]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref><ref name="jirin_門付">[[#外部リンク|''門付'']]、[[大辞林|大辞林 第三版]]、コトバンク、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>。表記は'''門付け'''（かどづけ）、読みは「かどつけ」とも<ref name="jisen_門付" />。'''門付する'''（かどづけする）という動詞としても使用し<ref name="jisen_門付" /><ref name="jirin_門付" />、芸については'''門付芸'''（かどづけげい）、行う者については'''門付芸人'''（かどづけげいにん）ともいう。多くは[[予祝芸能]]である。
== 歴史と概要 == 門付の発祥の根本には、季節に応じて神が祝福に訪れるという[[民間信仰]]があった<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" />。「祝言人」（ほかいびと）の芸能に由来するとも言われ、これは神を装い、民家の戸口等に立って祝福することば「祝い言」（ほかいごと）を発することで金銭を乞う者である<ref name="maipe_門付" />。[[平安時代]]、934年（[[承平 (日本)|承平]]4年）ころに成立したとされる『[[和名類聚抄]]』（934年ころ成立）には、「乞児」（ほかいびと）の文字で解説されており、[[乞食|物乞い]]であると10世紀の時点で定義されている<ref name="jisen_乞児">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>。
[[室町時代]]（[[14世紀]] - [[16世紀]]）には、寺に属しあるいは没落して民間に流れた職業芸人である[[声聞師]]（しょうもじ）が行った[[読経]]や[[曲舞]]等の芸能や<ref name="hyakka_声聞師">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、神社に隷属して雑役を行っていた下級の[[神人]]<ref name="hyakka_神人">{{Kotobank|神人|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>たちが、一定の季節に各戸を回って行っていた芸能である<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。これも次第に転落して、物乞いになっていった<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。この時代には、新春の予祝芸能である「[[千秋万歳]]」を声聞師らが行い<ref name="maipe_千秋万歳">{{Kotobank|千秋万歳|2=百科事典マイペディア}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく「[[松囃子]]」を声聞師のほか若党（[[武家奉公人]]）、[[町女房]]つまり一般町人の女性らが行い、平安時代、[[9世紀]]には存在したという漂流民「[[傀儡子]]」たちが、「[[人形回し]]」（操り人形による[[人形劇]]）等を行っていた<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。
[[ファイル:Manzai by unknown artist - wittig collection.jpg|thumb|200px|[[19世紀]]の「[[萬歳]]」のスタイル。]] [[江戸時代]]（[[17世紀]] - [[19世紀]]）には、正月に門口や座敷でその一家の予祝の祝言を謡う「[[萬歳]]」<ref name="hyakka_万歳">{{Kotobank|万歳|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく[[養蚕]]の予祝の祝言を謡う「[[春駒]]」<ref name="hyakka_春駒">{{Kotobank|春駒|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、同じく[[農耕]]の予祝の祝言を謡う「鳥追」（[[鳥追い]]）<ref name="hyakka_春駒" /><ref name="jisen_鳥追い">{{Kotobank|鳥追い|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>等の門付歌の類が広まった<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" />。萬歳は、千秋万歳の流れを汲むものであり<ref name="hyakka_万歳" />、鳥追は、江戸初期（17世紀）に京都・[[悲田院]]の[[与次郎]]（[[非人|非人頭]]のこと）が始めたとされ、江戸中期以降には、[[女太夫]]（[[非人]]、女性芸人）たちが[[日和下駄]]に[[編み笠]]のスタイルで[[三味線]]を弾きながら「鳥追唄」を歌いながらの門付を行うようになった<ref name="jisen_鳥追い" />。
正月の時期の門付は豊富で、[[大黒天]]の面・頭巾をかぶり、[[お福]]や[[えびす]]を伴って現れる「[[大黒舞]]」<ref name="hyakka_大黒舞">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、『[[古事記]]』にも記述がある古い芸能である「[[獅子舞]]」<ref name="hyakka_獅子舞">{{Kotobank|獅子舞|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、京都では巨大な張子、江戸では[[福禄寿]]の扮装をして歌い祝詞をあげる[[ちょろけん]]<ref name="jisen_ちょろけん">{{Kotobank|ちょろけん|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>といった、神々の姿をかたどったいでたちによるものがあり、現代でも一部地方では、これらの芸能は継承されている。
正月以外の時期の門付としては、[[節分]]・[[大晦日]]に厄年の人の家の門口に立った門付芸としての「[[厄払い]]」<ref name="jisen_厄払い">{{Kotobank|厄払い|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、[[師走]]に現れる芸人集団による「[[節季候]]」<ref name="hyakka_節季候">{{Kotobank|節季候|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、季節を問わず事件を詠み込み三味線で歌った「[[歌祭文]]」<ref name="jirin_歌祭文">{{Kotobank|歌祭文|2=大辞林 第三版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>、[[空也]]が始めたものを真似た「[[鉢叩]]」<ref name="hyakka_鉢叩">{{Kotobank|鉢叩|2=世界大百科事典 第2版}}、2012年8月8日閲覧。</ref>等が江戸時代を通じて広がった。町家のどこかで吉凶慶事があると集まるので、あまり来られて困る場合は、非人頭の善七または松右衛門の判が押してある「仕切り札」を門口に貼った<ref>[{{NDLDC|1042111/125}} 『江戸ばなし. 其2』][[三田村鳶魚]]、大東出版社、昭和18</ref>。門付は非人が行なっていたため、これが貼ってある場合は門付しないという仲間内の決まりになっていた。
[[明治|明治時代]]に新しく始まった門付に、「[[法界屋]]」が挙げられる<ref name="jisen_法界屋">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=デジタル大辞泉}}、2012年8月9日閲覧。</ref>。明治中期（[[1890年代]]ころ）に編み笠をかぶった[[書生]]が[[月琴]]に合わせて『[[法界節]]』を街頭で歌う[[巷間芸能]]が始められ、明治末期（[[1900年代]]ころ）には、大阪を中心に、[[琴]]・三味線・[[胡弓]]・[[尺八]]・[[太鼓]]といった多数の奏者とともに歌う門付となり、流行した<ref name="jisen_法界屋" />。また、[[沖縄]]でも、「京太郎」（[[チョンダラー]]）という門付があり、「萬歳」を詠い、本土の「傀儡子」のように「仏」（フトウキ）と呼ばれる人形回しを行い、門付した<ref name="maipe_チョンダラー">{{Kotobank|チョンダラー|2=百科事典マイペディア}}、2012年8月9日閲覧。</ref>。
門付の文化は、民間信仰に端を発したその発祥の時代にあっては、下級の宗教者が携わったが、次第に宗教者の行なう儀式や芸能を模倣して、市民生活の外側に置かれた貧しい者が担うようになった<ref name="hyakka_門付" />。その中から、『[[四つ竹節]]』<ref>楽器としての四ツ竹は、寄席に出演する江戸売り声の宮田章司が使用している</ref>や『法界節』のような[[俗謡]]が生まれている。{{See|#俗謡}} 門付の演じた夷・大黒天・お福・ちょろけん等のキャラクターは、京都の[[伏見人形]]をはじめとする各地の[[土人形]]にそのモチーフを提供した。
== おもな一覧 == 総称を列挙し、地域独特の呼称・習慣等は省く<ref name="hyakka_門付" /><ref name="maipe_門付" /><ref name="jisen_門付" /><ref name="jirin_門付" />。途絶えたものもあれば、これらの発展形が、門付ではない形で現在も各地民間に受け継がれているものもある。 * [[千秋万歳]] * [[松囃子]] * [[傀儡子]]（[[傀儡]]、[[人形回し]]、[[でこまわし]]） * [[萬歳]] - 門付歌 * [[春駒]] - 門付歌 * [[鳥追い|鳥追]] - 門付歌 * [[大黒舞]] * [[獅子舞]] * [[物吉]] * [[猿回し]] * [[ちょろけん]] * [[夷回し]]（[[夷舁き]]） * [[太々神楽]] * [[鹿島の事触れ]] * [[厄払い]] * [[夷舞]] * [[節季候]] * [[門浄瑠璃]] * [[門説経]] - 門付歌であり、[[説経節]]を門付芸として行うもの * [[歌祭文]] - 門付歌 * [[鉢叩]] - 門付歌 * [[なまいだ坊主]] * [[願人坊主]]（[[すたすた坊主]]） * [[芥太夫]] * [[簓摺り]] * [[法界屋]] * [[箱回し]] * [[化他]] * [[ちょぼくれ]] * [[胸叩き]] * [[チョンダラー]] - 沖縄の門付 * [[春田打ち]]
== 俗謡 == * [[四つ竹節]] * [[間の山節]] * [[博多節]] * [[串本節]] * [[新保広大寺]] * [[踊口説]] * [[法界節]] * [[新内節]]
== ギャラリー == <gallery> ファイル:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|猿曳（[[猿飼]]）、『[[洛中洛外図|洛中洛外図屏風]]』（[[1520年代]]、[[16世紀]]） ファイル:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|[[傀儡]]、『洛中洛外図屏風』（1520年代、16世紀） ファイル:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|[[鉢叩]]、『洛中洛外図屏風』（1520年代、16世紀） ファイル:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|[[鉦叩]]、『洛中洛外図屏風』（1520年代、16世紀） ファイル:Kadosekkyou.jpg|[[門説経]]、『人倫訓蒙図彙』（[[元禄]]年間） </gallery>

Created page with "'''Kadotsuke''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="..."
revision.text:
'''Kadotsuke''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. **New Year**: - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. **Year-End & Other Seasons**: - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
## See Also - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[Lion Dance]]
## References {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kadotsuke}} [[Category:Japanese Traditional Arts]] [[Category:Edo Period Entertainment]] [[Category:Street Performance]] [[Category:Japanese Folklore]]

'''Kadotsuke''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. **New Year**: - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. **Year-End & Other Seasons**: - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''Kadotsuke''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. **New Year**: - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. **Year-End & Other Seasons**: - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
## See Also - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[Lion Dance]]

'''Kadotsuke''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. **New Year**: - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. **Year-End & Other Seasons**: - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
## See Also - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[Lion Dance]]

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. **New Year**: - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. **Year-End & Other Seasons**: - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
## See Also - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[Lion Dance]]

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
## See Also - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[Lion Dance]]

## History and Overview Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
## Notable Performances ### Seasonal Performances 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
### Other Forms - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
## Cultural Impact Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
## Gallery <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
## See Also - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[Lion Dance]]

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == - [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] - [[Street Performances]] - [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kadotsuke]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kadotsuke]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:門付]]

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
