revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puppet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puppet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Clapper), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puqqet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puqqet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>

=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puqqet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puqqet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puqqet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puqqet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:I hope this works]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puqqet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Puqqet Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Claqqer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Puqqet manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>

These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>

=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>

Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery> == See Also! == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery> == See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery> == See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|12=draft}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).

'''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
