In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': - ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. - ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. - ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': - ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. - ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>

=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

<!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--> [[Category:Street performance]] [[Category:Japanese traditional performing arts]] [[Category:Burakumin]] [[Category:Performing arts in the Edo period]] [[Category:Gate performance]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

'''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

<!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--><!--Kadotsuke--> [[Category:Street performance]] [[Category:Japanese traditional performing arts]] [[Category:Burakumin]] [[Category:Performing arts in the Edo period]] [[Category:Gate performance]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11654943}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:門付]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />

In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:門付]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:門付]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:門付]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:門付]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:門付]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11654943]] Wikidata name: kadozuke [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>

'''{{ill|Kadotsuke|ja|門付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadotsuke|qq=}}''' (門付, かどづけ) is a type of traditional Japanese street performance characterized by performers standing at gates or entrances of homes to showcase their art in exchange for monetary or material offerings.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition}}</ref><ref name="maipe_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=MyPedia Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="jisen_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref><ref name="jirin_kadotsuke">{{Kotobank|門付|2=Daijirin 3rd Edition}}</ref> The term is also used to refer to the performers themselves, and their performances are known as ''kadotsuke-gei'' (門付芸).
Kadotsuke is often linked with traditional celebratory and ritualistic arts (予祝芸能), aimed at invoking blessings for households.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" />
== History and Overview == Kadotsuke originated from folk beliefs wherein deities were believed to visit homes to bestow blessings during specific seasons.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" /> Early forms of kadotsuke were tied to performers known as "hokai-bito" (祝言人), who mimicked deities, offered blessings, and requested alms.<ref name="maipe_kadotsuke" />
The practice has roots as far back as the Heian period (794–1185), where references to similar performers can be found in historical texts like the ''Wamyō Ruijushō'' (934).<ref name="jisen_hokai">{{Kotobank|乞児|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref> Over time, various forms of kadotsuke emerged, often carried out by marginalized groups such as ''shōmoji'' (声聞師) and lower-Ranking shrine workers (神人).<ref name="hyakka_shoumoji">{{Kotobank|声聞師|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref><ref name="hyakka_shinjin">{{Kotobank|神人|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
In the Edo period (1603–1868), kadotsuke flourished with performances like ''manzai'' (萬歳) for New Year blessings, ''harukoma'' (春駒) for sericulture prosperity, and ''tori-oi'' (鳥追) for agricultural fortune.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> New Year performances also included ''daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞) and ''shishimai'' (獅子舞). These performances often involved elaborate costumes representing deities and mythological figures.
== Notable Performances ==
=== Seasonal Performances === 1. '''New Year''': *** ''Manzai'': Ritualistic blessings with songs and dance. *** ''Shishimai'': Lion dances to ward off evil spirits. *** ''Daikoku-mai'': Performers dressed as Daikoku, Ebisu, and other deities.<ref name="hyakka_daikokumai">{{Kotobank|大黒舞|2=World Encyclopedia}}</ref>
2. '''Year-End & Other Seasons''': *** ''Setsubun'' or ''Yaku-barai'': Performed to drive away bad luck. *** ''Sekki-goe'': Winter performances requesting seasonal alms.
=== Other Forms === - ''Kairaishi'' (傀儡子): Pu11et manipulation acts.<ref name="hyakka_kadotsuke" /> - ''Tori-oi'': Celebratory songs for agricultural blessings. - ''Hōkai-bushi'': Ballads originating in the Meiji period (1868–1912).<ref name="jisen_houkaiya">{{Kotobank|法界屋|2=Digital Daijisen}}</ref>
== Cultural Impact == Kadotsuke performances were instrumental in the creation of popular folk songs (俗謡) such as ''Hōkai-bushi'' and ''Yottsutake-bushi''. Characters and motifs from kadotsuke performances inspired local crafts, including ''Fushimi dolls'' (伏見人形).
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Saruhiki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Saruhiki'' (Monkey Trainer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' (16th century) File:Kugutu Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kairaishi'' (Pu11et Performer), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Hachitataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Hachitataki'' (Bowl Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' File:Kanetataki Motonobu Rakuchu-Rakugai.jpg|''Kanetataki'' (Gong Cla11er), from the ''Rakuchū Rakugai Zu'' </gallery>
== See Also == * [[Japanese Traditional Performing Arts]] * [[Street Performances]] * {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance|1=Lion dance}}
